message:
security(mcp): log policy-denied MCP mutations via tracing (ft-6mmyp)

a4b7c8c3's mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation returned MCP_ERR_POLICY on
Deny/RequireApproval and returned early — leaving NO observability signal
on denied attempts. The wa.events_annotate path at mcp_tools.rs:4330-4352
audits SUCCESSFUL mutations; denied mutations had neither an audit row
nor a log emission. Operators reviewing a burst of suspicious MCP
activity couldn't correlate client attempts against the audit log — the
denied attempts simply weren't in it.

Add structured tracing::warn! on both Deny and RequireApproval branches
under target `ft::security::policy`, with tool / command / decision /
rule_id / reason fields. An operator running with
`RUST_LOG=ft::security::policy=warn` (or a SIEM tailing journald by that
target) now sees every denied MCP mutation in real time with enough
structure to pivot on the responsible rule_id.

MVP scope intentionally: the bead's full fix (record_audit_action_redacted
into audit_actions) would require either an async helper or open-storage-
in-sync-path machinery the current gate doesn't have. That deeper fix is
filed as ft-h90rh with a concrete refactor path (GateOutcome enum so
callers own the audit write + envelope return).

No change to the returned error envelope shape or code (MCP_ERR_POLICY)
— existing callers and tests continue to see the same response on deny.

Agent: RusticMeadow

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-682n6","title":"Migrate ~50 tokio-based async tests to asupersync runtime (ft-22x4r tracked)","description":"# Goal
Convert ~50 tests using #[tokio::test] to work with the asupersync runtime. These tests fail at runtime with: task::spawn called outside of Runtime::block_on context. Originally tracked as ft-22x4r.

# Background
The asupersync migration replaced tokio, but ~50 tests still use #[tokio::test]. Count verified 2026-04-06: 50 in .rs files (excluding .bak). Most are in *_labruntime.rs test files (2 per file across ~25 files) plus 2 in dependency_eradication.rs.

# Design
## Discovery (exact count at implementation time)
1. grep -r '#\[tokio::test\]' crates/ --include='*.rs' | grep -v '.bak' > /tmp/tokio_tests.txt
2. Count and categorize by file pattern:
   - *_labruntime.rs files: ~25 files × 2 tests = ~50 tests (async smoke + basic scenario)
   - dependency_eradication.rs: 2 tests
3. For each, identify tokio primitives used: spawn, sleep, channels, timeout

## Migration strategy
- *_labruntime.rs pattern: These all follow the same template (2 tests per file). Once one is migrated, the rest are mechanical.
  Replace: #[tokio::test] with #[test] + runtime_compat::block_on(async { ... })
  Or: if asupersync provides a test macro, use that
- Replace tokio::spawn -> runtime_compat spawn equivalent
- Replace tokio::time::sleep -> runtime_compat::sleep
- Replace tokio channels -> runtime_compat channels

## Batch approach
Since *_labruntime.rs files follow a uniform pattern, write a migration helper that does the mechanical replacement, then verify each file compiles.

## Structured Logging
- Migration log: file, old_macro, new_macro, primitives_replaced, compile_status

# Acceptance Criteria
1. Zero #[tokio::test] in .rs files (verified by grep, excluding .bak).
2. All ~50 previously-failing tests now pass.
3. No semantic changes — same assertions.
4. Total test count preserved.
5. No new tokio dependencies.
6. Commit message lists affected files.

# Implementation Notes
- runtime_compat.rs provides bridge primitives.
- The *_labruntime.rs naming convention suggests these are lab/smoke tests for async behavior.
- This bead unblocks ft-xbnl0.2 (async cutover epic).
- Coordinate with ft-n2j5w (MCP test fix) if any MCP tests also use tokio::test.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":240,"created_at":"2026-04-06T18:58:21.227738Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-07T02:55:15.155157Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T02:55:15.154811Z","close_reason":"Verified: zero actual #[tokio::test] annotations remain in crates/frankenterm-core/. All 50 occurrences found by grep are in comments/docstrings/string literals describing the migration that was already done. The *_labruntime.rs files already use #[test] + RuntimeFixture::current_thread().block_on(). No code changes needed.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","migration","testing"]}
 {"id":"ft-6gqga","title":"[stub] workflows/wait_execution.rs:199-204: WaitCondition::External permanently returns Unsupported","description":"# Genuine production stub

File: `crates/frankenterm-core/src/workflows/wait_execution.rs:199-204` — `WaitConditionExecutor::execute_wait` arm for `WaitCondition::External { key }`.

```rust
WaitCondition::External { key } => {
    // External signals are not implemented in this layer
    Ok(WaitConditionResult::Unsupported {
        reason: format!("External signal '{key}' requires external signal registry"),
    })
}
```

This is not a platform stub (the pattern-wait and timeout-wait arms above it are fully implemented) — `External` is a first-class variant of the public `WaitCondition` enum but the execution path has NEVER been wired. Every caller that constructs `WaitCondition::External { key }` silently gets `Unsupported` back. The error hint names 'external signal registry' as a missing dependency, but no such registry exists anywhere in the crate (`rg 'external.*signal.*registry' crates/frankenterm-core/src` returns zero hits).

# Scope — NOT the same as the frankensqlite family

Recorder/ingest/reindex FrankenSqlite stubs are tracked under the BLOCKED wa-2l27x.8 FrankenSQLite migration plan and were narratively disclosed via ft-trby3. The `WaitCondition::External` stub has no parent bead — it's an orphaned production gap.

# Fix options

1. **Remove the variant.** If no caller actually constructs `External`, the enum should not expose the variant. Git blame shows the variant was added speculatively; `rg 'WaitCondition::External' crates` finds only the match arm and the enum declaration — no producer. Cleanest fix: delete the variant + match arm, file the follow-up when an external-signal feature is actually designed.

2. **Implement the registry.** Introduce a `SignalRegistry` trait with pub fn `signal(key: &str)` / `wait(key: &str, timeout: Duration) -> future`. Thread it through `WaitConditionExecutor::new`. Non-trivial — requires designing signal lifetime/TTL semantics.

Recommend option (1) pending a real consumer. Either way, `WaitConditionResult::Unsupported` has no legitimate emit site after the fix.

# Acceptance

Either `WaitCondition::External` is removed from the public enum + all tests updated, OR the executor actually waits on a registered external signal and the integration is covered by a test that registers, fires, and observes the signal.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T05:45:23.924074Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T06:06:29.076549Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T06:06:29.076246Z","close_reason":"Fixed in ef9c7895","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

-{"id":"ft-6mmyp","title":"[review][MEDIUM] ft-x86z2 gate returns MCP_ERR_POLICY on Deny/RequireApproval without recording an audit action","description":"Proactive policy.rs audit round 20. a4b7c8c3's mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation helper at mcp_tools.rs:177-206 returns an MCP_ERR_POLICY error envelope on Deny or RequireApproval and returns early — WITHOUT calling storage.record_audit_action_redacted or any equivalent. Effect: a policy deny on wa.tx_run, wa.tx_rollback, wa.mission_pause, wa.mission_resume, or wa.mission_abort leaves NO audit trail. Operators reviewing a suspicious activity burst cannot correlate MCP client attempts against the audit log — the denied attempts are not in it. Compare wa.events_annotate at mcp_tools.rs:4330-4352 which writes an AuditActionRecord AFTER the mutation. Successful mutations are audited; denied mutations are not. That asymmetry defeats the purpose of a security audit log. Fix: extend mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation to take the storage handle and record an AuditActionRecord with result=denied and decision_reason=policy_reason(decision) before returning. Or write a parallel log stream dedicated to policy-denied attempts. Related ft-eu0no HIGH (rate limiter ineffective) means the missing audit is especially bad because denies that should happen via rate limiter also never happen. Severity MEDIUM: security-observability gap, not a direct exploit, but blocks forensics on any real attack. Audit round 20, 2026-04-23.  {"id":"ft-6mxfs","title":"[mor:security-engineering] ws_dir = root.join('.ft','patterns') is not canonicalized before discovery","description":"# Observation

In `load_packs_from_config`
(crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs:949):

    let ws_dir = root_path.join(".ft").join("patterns");
    let discovered = discover_packs_from_dir(&ws_dir)?;

ft-fbee5 (just closed) fixed the case where a symlink INSIDE
`.ft/patterns/` escapes the sandbox. But the parent path is itself
just a join, no canonicalize. A hostile cloned repo can ship:

    .ft/patterns -> /etc

(a symlink at the directory level.) `ws_dir` canonicalizes to
`/etc`, `discover_packs_from_dir(/etc)` iterates there, and the
per-file sandbox check passes because it canonicalizes with root =
`/etc`. The symlink escape is now one level up.

Similar attack surface for `config.resolved_user_packs_dir()` if
that ever returns a joined path the caller doesn't canonicalize.

# Question

Should `load_packs_from_config` canonicalize `ws_dir` and verify the
result resolves *inside* `root_path.canonicalize()` before calling
`discover_packs_from_dir`? That closes the parent-level symlink
vector without needing to change `discover_packs_from_dir`'s
contract.

Stretch: is there a case for centralizing this as a
`sandbox_resolve_dir(dir, root)` helper and using it everywhere the
pack/workflow/policy loaders join a workspace-relative path? The
pattern is probably not unique to pack loading.

# Trust boundary

The repo is the attacker; the user's home directory is the victim.
Everything a hostile repo can make `read_to_string` see is a
potential data-exfil primitive. Treat repo-relative path
construction as crossing a trust boundary, and canonicalize at the
boundary crossing, not at the leaf.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:35:09.320199Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:49.174562Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:51:59.305207Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 0239fbd6 — added sandbox_resolve_dir helper, routed load_packs_from_config's ws_dir through it with 4 regression tests (missing, inside-root, toplevel-symlink-escape, end-to-end).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mor","sandboxing","security"]}
 {"id":"ft-6piwt","title":"[P0 BLOCKER] frankenterm-core lib has 61 compile errors under --no-default-features — blocks all --no-default-features test/bench runs","description":"## Symptom
`cargo check -p frankenterm-core --lib --no-default-features` fails with 61 errors. Panes 3 and 4 in the swarm both hit this while trying to verify their respective commits.

## Representative error
```
error[E0599]: no method named `record_event_with_cx` found for this struct
  --> crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs:6157
  help: there is a method `record_event` with a similar name

error[E0599]: no method named `get_text_with_cx` found for struct `std::sync::Arc<(dyn WeztermInterface + 'static)>`
  --> crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs:3272:43
  note: `wezterm::PaneTextSource` defines an item `get_text_with_cx`, perhaps you need to implement it
```

## Root cause hypothesis
Asupersync migration landed `_with_cx` **callers** in some files without landing `_with_cx` **implementations** on the corresponding traits/structs, specifically under the default feature set (where the legacy impls are gated off but the new impls aren't gated on).

## Scope
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs (record_event_with_cx)
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs (get_text_with_cx + PaneTextSource trait alignment)
- 61 errors total; likely same pattern elsewhere

## Fix direction
1. Run `cargo check -p frankenterm-core --lib --no-default-features 2>&1 | grep 'error\[\|error:' > /tmp/errors.txt` to enumerate all 61.
2. Group by method name; for each missing `*_with_cx` method:
   - If the non-cx variant exists, add a default impl or route `with_cx` through it (respects cancel semantics).
   - If neither exists, revert the caller to the non-cx variant with a TODO referencing ft-xbnl0.2 epic.
3. Verify with `cargo check` clean, then `cargo test --lib --no-default-features`.

## Priority
P0 — blocks swarm from running any tests under --no-default-features, which is the recommended lean test surface.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T14:29:56.725238Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T14:50:22.680762Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T14:50:22.680537Z","close_reason":"Added no-feature cx compatibility shims and ungated fallback *_with_cx adapters so frankenterm-core --lib --no-default-features compiles again","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-eqtcq","title":"aegis_entropy_anomaly::EProcess::update propagates NaN e_value; permanently disables rejection","description":"EProcess::update calls likelihood_ratio(h, baseline_mean, baseline_std, collapse_mean, collapse_std). If any f64 input is NaN, log_h0 and log_h1 both become NaN, log_lr = NaN - NaN = NaN, log_lr.exp() = NaN, and clamp(1e-6, 1e6) passes NaN through because f64::clamp does not reject NaN. self.e_value *= NaN = NaN. The final clamp(1e-100, max_e) at update:226 also passes NaN through. Future is_rejected() returns (NaN >= threshold) = false, silently disabling anomaly detection for the lifetime of this EProcess. Matches ft-b4l62 / ft-yskcu / ft-c17iw / ft-n4fdx pattern — fail-closed on non-finite inputs at the update() boundary.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T18:03:02.334443Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T18:05:25.492018Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T18:05:25.491748Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-eze48","title":"[fuzz] add FtviIndex::from_bytes harness for vector index loader","description":"The FTVI vector-index binary loader is public and currently unfuzzed.

Evidence:
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/search/mod.rs:73` publicly re-exports `FtviIndex`.
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/search/vector_index.rs:218-267` parses attacker-controlled bytes in `FtviIndex::from_bytes`, including magic/version checks, dimension/count headers, and per-record half-float decoding loops.
- `fuzz/Cargo.toml` has no target for `FtviIndex::from_bytes`.

Why it matters:
- This is a binary format loader over persisted search state, so malformed bytes can hit allocation pressure, unexpected EOF handling, half-float edge cases, and count/dimension loops.
- The surface is already public, synchronous, and easy to fuzz without extra seams.

Fix:
- Add a fuzz target that exercises both arbitrary raw bytes and structured valid FTVI buffers via `write_ftvi_vec` round-trips, asserting parse no-panics and round-trip sanity where applicable.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","estimated_minutes":30,"created_at":"2026-04-23T19:26:37.331896Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T19:44:04.556262Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T19:44:04.555662Z","close_reason":"Shipped in 6567397b: add FtviIndex::from_bytes fuzz harness","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fuzz","review"]}

 {"id":"ft-h8da2","title":"[security][P3] Verify Redactor is applied on every pane-content read path (design-audit)","description":"## Context

Pane content is stored UNREDACTED at write time: crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs:12363 append_segment_sync inserts the raw content string directly into output_segments.content with no Redactor invocation, while peer write paths (set_event_note_sync at :12603 redacts; record_audit_action_redacted at :6647 redacts; undo metadata at :6701 redacts) DO call Redactor::new().redact(...).

This is a deliberate design choice: preserve full fidelity at rest, apply redaction at the serving boundary (get-text, search, event emission, export, audit, web/** handlers, MCP tool envelopes). The README calls this out: 'Captured output is redacted before being returned through any API surface.'

The design is sound but fragile: every read path must redact. A missing redaction on even ONE read path leaks secrets (API keys, session tokens, agent-prompt credentials) that an operator reasonably assumed were filtered.

## Scope of this task

NOT a code change. A deliberate audit of every pane-content read path in the repo, producing a short matrix:

| Read path | File:line | Redacts? |
|-----------|-----------|----------|

Paths to check (non-exhaustive):
- wa.get_text MCP tool (mcp_tools.rs)
- wa.search MCP tool (mcp_tools.rs)
- ft robot get-text CLI (main.rs)
- ft robot search CLI (main.rs)
- /panes /events /search /stream/* web handlers (web/handlers.rs, web/sse.rs)
- ft export (export.rs)
- ft audit / ft history (storage access paths)
- ft replay (replay.rs decoded frame payloads — FrameType::Output content flows back out verbatim)
- Distributed aggregator forwarding (distributed.rs)

Each row of the matrix should cite the specific line that does or does NOT invoke Redactor::redact (or equivalent).

## Why P3

Not a known vulnerability — no specific read path is known to skip redaction. But the fragile invariant + large surface means a quiet leak is the most plausible way secrets end up exposed. Cheap to verify; one-time cost.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Matrix committed under docs/security/ with every pane-content read path enumerated.
- Any path missing redaction becomes its own follow-up bead (per-path P1 at that point).
- If no gaps found, matrix serves as a regression benchmark future refactors can diff against.

+{"id":"ft-h90rh","title":"[security][follow-up ft-6mmyp] Persist policy-denied MCP mutations to audit_actions table (not just tracing log)","description":"## Context

ft-6mmyp primary fix shipped: mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation now emits structured tracing::warn! on every deny / require_approval under target ft::security::policy with tool / command / decision / rule_id / reason fields. That closes the 'zero observability' gap for denied MCP mutations.

The bead's ideal (fuller) fix was a record_audit_action_redacted write so denied attempts appear in the same audit_actions table as successful ones — enables SQL-queryable forensics and parity with the wa.events_annotate post-mutation audit at mcp_tools.rs:4330-4352.

## Why deferred

record_audit_action_redacted is async. The current sync gate helper at mcp_tools.rs:177-231 can't await. The callers (tx_run/rollback, mission_pause/resume/abort) all do already live inside a runtime.block_on scope, but the helper is invoked BEFORE that block_on — specifically to fail fast without opening a StorageHandle. Opening storage inside the helper on the deny path would:
  - Open a new StorageHandle (~100ms cold path)
  - Require passing db_path (currently the helper only takes &Config)
  - Add a runtime.block_on inside a sync fn that may already be inside a tokio runtime → panic risk

## Fix path

Either:
(a) Push the audit write into the deny branch of each of the 5 caller handlers (they already have runtime.block_on scopes) — duplicates ~10 lines per caller but avoids helper signature changes.
(b) Refactor mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation to return a deny-decision value and have the caller write the audit row plus the error envelope — single point of responsibility per tool.
(c) Change the helper signature to async fn mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation(config, db_path, ...) -> ... and have callers await it inside their existing runtime.block_on.

(b) is cleanest. Roughly:

    enum GateOutcome {
        Allow,
        Deny { envelope: McpResult<Vec<Content>>, audit: AuditActionRecord },
        RequireApproval { envelope: McpResult<Vec<Content>>, audit: AuditActionRecord },
    }
    fn mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation(config, summary, command_text, start) -> GateOutcome

Callers match, write the audit via their existing storage in the deny/approval branches, then return the envelope.

## Acceptance Criteria

- After deny/approval, audit_actions has a row with result='denied' or 'require_approval', decision_reason=policy_reason, action_kind matching the tool.
- Integration test per tool: seed a deny-config, call tool, assert audit row shape.
- Tracing log emission (shipped under ft-6mmyp main fix) remains in place as a second signal.

 {"id":"ft-ybtyg","title":"[MEDIUM] session checkpoint + snapshot files have no integrity check; 'BLAKE3' docs vs SipHash reality","description":"Found during /security-audit-for-saas pass #5 (pane 5, 2026-04-21).

## Summary
Two related gaps in the session-persistence trust story:

1. Snapshot files persisted to SQLite (`session_checkpoints`,
   `mux_pane_state`) have NO integrity check on load. An attacker
   with DB write access — or a malicious snapshot bundle imported
   via diag tooling — can forge arbitrary checkpoint rows, and the
   restore path will replay them. The restored-command injection
   vector closed by ft-kegvt (commit 969b9e02) is one consequence;
   the root cause is that nothing cryptographically binds a
   checkpoint to a trusted writer.

2. Documentation drift amplifies the problem: snapshot_engine.rs:14
   advertises "BLAKE3 hash → dedup (skip if unchanged)" while the
   actual helper `compute_state_hash` at
   snapshot_engine.rs:1641-1651 uses
   `std::collections::hash_map::DefaultHasher` (SipHash-24, not a
   cryptographic hash). Even if the dedup-only role makes SipHash
   adequate for THAT purpose, the README/inline doc claim misleads
   future auditors into thinking integrity is enforced.

## Evidence

### Missing integrity verification
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/session_restore.rs:466-474 and
  :509-517: `INSERT INTO session_checkpoints (..., state_hash,
  ...)` with the literal string `'restore'` as the state_hash
  column value. No hash is computed, stored, or later verified.
- storage.rs:609 schema comment claims `state_hash TEXT NOT NULL,
  -- BLAKE3 of serialized state for dedup`, but the INSERT sites
  (:2031, :2268, :2589) either pass a computed SipHash hex string
  (from `compute_state_hash`) or the hardcoded `'restore'`.
- grep confirms zero `verify_snapshot`, `snapshot_hash`,
  `hmac`, `HMAC`, or `verify.*checkpoint` sites across
  `snapshot_engine.rs` + `session_restore.rs`. Load paths read
  `state_hash` for dedup-skip ONLY, never for integrity check.

### Documentation drift
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/snapshot_engine.rs:14 — the module
  docstring says:
      `├── BLAKE3 hash  → dedup (skip if unchanged)`
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/snapshot_engine.rs:1641-1651:
      ```rust
      fn compute_state_hash(panes: &[PaneInfo]) -> String {
          use std::hash::{Hash, Hasher};
          let mut hasher =
              std::collections::hash_map::DefaultHasher::new();
          ...
          ids.hash(&mut hasher);
          ...
          format!("{:016x}", hasher.finish())
      }
      ```
  That's SipHash-24 formatted as a 16-hex-char u64. Not BLAKE3.
  No `blake3::` / `sha2::` imports in snapshot_engine.rs.

## Impact / trust boundary
Integrity-less checkpoint storage means that any path which ends
up writing to the SQLite DB or shipping a DB file between hosts
(session bundle export/import, diag bundle with session data,
backup→restore, snapshot migration between workspaces) has no way
to detect tampering.

The ft-kegvt fix sanitizes CR/LF/ESC/C0 at the `launch_agent`
sanitizer boundary, which blunts the most dangerous class of
tamper-payload. But it doesn't stop an attacker from, e.g.,
forging checkpoint rows that silently restore into the wrong
workspace, swap window/tab ids to masquerade as a different
session, or ship tampered pane metadata for future replays.

Severity: MEDIUM. Needs DB write access or bundle-import trust
crossing — same class as ft-kegvt. Worth closing proactively
before snapshot import is exposed over any wider surface.

## Fix direction

1. Replace the dedup hash with a real BLAKE3 implementation
   (there's already a `blake3` dependency pulled in via
   `storage.rs` + `codec`). Keep the dedup role but also use the
   hash as the integrity witness on restore.
2. Drop the hardcoded `'restore'` placeholder at session_restore.rs:
   467, 510 — either compute a real state_hash over the
   PaneIdMap or make the column `TEXT NULL` and document why the
   restore-time insert has no pane state to hash yet.
3. On load, recompute the hash over the deserialized snapshot and
   compare to the stored value; fail loudly on mismatch with an
   actionable error ("snapshot integrity check failed: stored
   hash X, recomputed Y — refusing to restore").
4. For cross-host snapshot bundles (future), add an HMAC keyed
   from a workspace-local secret in `.ft/hmac.key` so bundles
   cannot be silently swapped between workspaces.
5. Fix the docstring drift at snapshot_engine.rs:14 in lockstep
   with whichever real hash gets adopted.

## Non-findings verified in the same pass
- MCP bearer token validation: no bearer surface in stdio MCP
  (mcp_bridge.rs). Auth is out-of-band via OS process context.
  Clean.
- `wa.send` approval enforcement in non-TTY: mcp_tools.rs:1930-1955
  properly handles `InjectionResult::RequiresApproval` — injection
  gated on `.is_allowed()` at line 1955. Approval-required actions
  return the envelope back, do not auto-bypass. Clean.
- Distributed mode token validation: `constant_time_eq` at
  distributed.rs:209-222 is correctly implemented. `validate_token`
  at :248-289 uses it for both identity and secret. Clean.
- Agent-mail contact handshake: out of scope per AGENTS.md rule
  (NEVER touch agent-mail service). Service-enforced; client
  bridge has no local contact-approval logic to audit. Out of
  scope by policy.

## Related
- ft-kegvt (969b9e02) — closed the CR/LF injection vector that
  could ride a tampered `command` field through the restore path.
  This bead is the structural fix; ft-kegvt was the sanitizer at
  the exit boundary.
- ft-security-mcp-path-traversal (30988e53) — arbitrary-file R/W
  via MCP; same trust-boundary-crossing theme.
- ft-xv561 (070117a0) — ReDoS via user-pack regexes.

 {"id":"ft-ych74","title":"[review][LOW] release.yml smoke test excludes windows-latest — ft-windows-amd64.exe ships without --version/--help/doctor probe","description":".github/workflows/release.yml:68-80 gates the Smoke test binary step with:

    if: matrix.target == 'x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu' || startsWith(matrix.target, 'aarch64-apple') || startsWith(matrix.target, 'x86_64-apple')

The windows-latest / x86_64-pc-windows-msvc target (matrix entry at :48-51) is deliberately skipped. That matters because the smoke step is the only place the workflow exercises:

    "$BINARY" --version
    "$BINARY" version --full
    "$BINARY" --help
    "$BINARY" doctor --json

Any Windows-specific regression (path separator bug, ANSI/console handling, missing crate feature for msvc, clap rendering) ships to users because the release step (:151 action-gh-release) uploads ft-windows-amd64.exe without ever invoking it.

aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu is also skipped (the condition only matches x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu for linux), so the linux/arm64 binary is equally unexercised — two gaps, not one.

Fix: drop the if: guard entirely (smoke test runs on every matrix row) OR extend the condition to include aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu and x86_64-pc-windows-msvc, adjusting the shell to pwsh on windows (Compress-Archive uses pwsh already at :93 so the pattern exists).

Severity: LOW — pre-launch for v0.1.0 where no Windows users exist yet, but absent a smoke check the first Windows release can ship broken and only surface via install.sh failures in the field.

-{"id":"ft-yj375","title":"[security][P1] wa.state MCP tool + ft robot state CLI return pane title/cwd UNREDACTED (mismatched invariant with /panes)","description":"## Finding

Audit under ft-h8da2 (docs/security/read-path-redaction-matrix.md) confirms: wa.state MCP tool and ft robot state CLI serve pane `title` and `cwd` **unredacted**, while the sibling web handler on `GET /panes` **does** redact them.

### Evidence

- crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_types.rs:817-834 — McpPaneState::from_pane_info:

      title: info.title.clone(),
      cwd: info.cwd.clone(),

- crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:5501-5518 — PaneState::from_pane_info (CLI): same pattern, same unredacted clone.

- crates/frankenterm-core/src/web/handlers.rs:64-70 — PaneView::from_record (web): **CORRECT** reference implementation:

      title: r.title.map(|t| redactor.redact(&t)),
      cwd: r.cwd.map(|c| redactor.redact(&c)),

### Threat

Pane titles are set by whatever process the pane hosts. Agents that bake credentials into the title (e.g. `codex --api-key sk-ant-...` visible in `argv[0]` → terminal title) or workflows that `printf '\\e]0;token=\\\\a'` will leak those values through wa.state and ft robot state.

A peer MCP agent that calls wa.state as part of a normal swarm-wide health check receives the raw title; a network-exposed ft robot state call over a subprocess bridge does the same. Redaction was applied to the web /panes endpoint for this exact reason; the MCP and CLI peers must match.

### Fix

Two equivalent options:

Option A (one-line per call-site, web-matching): in the wa.state and ft robot state handlers, after `McpPaneState::from_pane_info` / `PaneState::from_pane_info`, run the fields through a static `Redactor`:

    let redactor = Redactor::new();
    for s in &mut states {
        if let Some(t) = s.title.as_mut() { *t = redactor.redact(t); }
        if let Some(c) = s.cwd.as_mut()   { *c = redactor.redact(c); }
    }

Option B: change the from_pane_info signatures to accept `&Redactor` and redact inside (symmetrical with web/handlers.rs:64-70).

Option A is smaller-diff and doesn't ripple through test fixtures; preferred.

### Acceptance Criteria

- wa.state envelope serializes title/cwd through Redactor::redact.
- ft robot state envelope serializes title/cwd through Redactor::redact.
- Regression test: seed a pane with title containing `sk-ant-api03-XXXX`, invoke both surfaces, assert the raw token does not appear in the response JSON.
- docs/security/read-path-redaction-matrix.md wa.state + ft robot state rows flip from `✗ LEAK` to `✓`.

### Why P1

Real leak on the default configuration — no operator opt-in required. Blast radius is narrow (titles/cwds, not pane scrollback) but automatic (fleet-wide state poll). Fix is mechanical.

 {"id":"ft-yjt9e","title":"policy::RateLimiter::pane_counts leaks entries forever — no eviction on pane destroy or GC path","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:2076` — `RateLimiter.pane_counts: HashMap<(u64, ActionKind), Vec<Instant>>`. Every unique `(pane_id, action)` pair `check()` sees creates an entry. Nothing removes them.

A grep confirms it:

    $ rg 'pane_counts\.(remove|clear|retain)|fn gc|fn cleanup|fn prune_panes' crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs
    (no matches)

## Failure shape

On a long-running ft watcher with high pane churn (the 200+-pane fleet the project is designed for), every closed pane leaves a stale `(pane_id, action)` entry in `pane_counts` — one per ActionKind the pane ever touched. Over weeks those entries accumulate:

- Each entry holds a `Vec<Instant>` of up to `limit_per_pane` entries (default 60, configurable up to u32::MAX).
- After the sliding window expires, `prune_old` empties the Vec on the next `check()` — but only if check() is called with that (pane_id, action) again, which never happens for a destroyed pane.
- The entry itself is never removed, so the HashMap grows monotonically.

For 10k destroyed panes × 5 action kinds × 16B-per-Instant × 60-entries-peak = ~48 MB of leaked state. Not a crash, but a silent drift that scales with fleet uptime.

Global side (`global_counts: HashMap<ActionKind, Vec<Instant>>`) is BOUNDED by the finite ActionKind enum — not affected.

# Fix

Add two method shapes to `impl RateLimiter`:

1. `remove_pane(&mut self, pane_id: u64)` — iterate pane_counts, retain only keys whose first element ≠ pane_id. Called from the runtime's PaneDestroyed handler (same handler that already calls `remove_runtime_pane_state_for_pane` in runtime.rs:3574).

2. `gc(&mut self)` (or `gc_at(&mut self, now: Instant)` for testability) — iterate pane_counts, for each entry call prune_old with now-window as cutoff, drop the whole entry if the resulting Vec is empty. Safe opportunistic cleanup that catches panes closed without an explicit PaneDestroyed signal (crashes, abrupt detaches).

Also add operational telemetry: `pane_entries_evicted: u64` counter so operators can see the leak being managed.

# Tests

Inline `#[cfg(test)]` in policy.rs:

- `rate_limiter_remove_pane_drops_all_action_entries_for_that_pane`
- `rate_limiter_remove_pane_leaves_other_panes_intact`
- `rate_limiter_gc_drops_expired_empty_entries`
- `rate_limiter_gc_preserves_still-active_entries`

# Severity

MEDIUM — leak is slow, bounded per pane, doesn't panic. But it's cumulative and silent. Fleet operators would only see the symptom as gradual RSS creep over uptime-weeks, attributed to anything-but-rate-limiter. Shipping the eviction path closes the hole before someone has to bisect it from production.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:51:26.703056Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:57:11.104139Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:57:11.103827Z","close_reason":"Landed as part of 27ef45e1 (absorbed commit includes ft-yjt9e methods: RateLimiter::remove_pane, gc, gc_at, tracked_pane_entry_count + 4 regression tests: remove_pane_drops_all_action_entries_for_that_pane, remove_pane_leaves_global_counts_intact, gc_drops_expired_empty_entries, gc_preserves_still_active_entries).  {"id":"ft-ym8nq","title":"[review][LOW] ft-x86z2 policy gate on 5 mutating MCP tools ships without deny-path regression tests","description":"a4b7c8c3 added mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation gate at mcp_tools.rs:177-206 and wired it into 5 mutators (wa.tx_run/tx_rollback/mission_pause/resume/abort). Gate placement verified BEFORE any side effect (before mcp_resolve_mission_tx_file_path which canonicalizes fs paths; before contract/mission load; before state persist). Happy-path + allow-default tests continue to pass. But the commit explicitly defers deny-path coverage: 'Deny-policy tests per tool are scoped into a separate pass.'

Effect: the gate helper is not fenced by any test that exercises the is_denied() / requires_approval() branches. A future refactor of PolicyEngine's decision variants (or a rename of MCP_ERR_POLICY, or a change to elapsed_ms semantics in the error envelope, or a regression that breaks the early-return in the helper) would silently turn the gate into a no-op. All 5 production call sites would compile and tests would still pass, because every test path runs through an allow decision.

Scope of missing tests (5 tools x 2 deny modes = 10 missing tests, or one parameterized test per mode):
  - wa.tx_run deny -> assert envelope.ok=false, error_code=MCP_ERR_POLICY, no side effect (contract not loaded, tx not executed)
  - wa.tx_run require_approval -> assert envelope.ok=false, error_code=MCP_ERR_POLICY, hint contains 'allow-once'
  - ...same for wa.tx_rollback, wa.mission_pause/_resume/_abort

Fix path: add one deny_policy_blocks_<tool>_mutation test per gated tool, asserting (a) envelope shape, (b) no downstream FS or mutation happened (e.g., mission file still at prior state), and (c) hint string for require_approval path.

Severity LOW — not a regression today. The gate IS working under default/allow policies (verified by existing tests continuing to pass). Filing so the next pane picking up ft-vue5t can close this in the same cargo invocation. Audit round 19, 2026-04-23.
